author: @vectron50444
text: One thing you have to remember when it comes to "unnecessary" acrobatics in lightsaber combat is that in lore/legends especially, the dueling is significantly faster  than you see on screen. Those acrobatics are designed to make it harder to track your blade and keep the enemy guessing where you're heading next. Yoda v. Dooku on Geonosis is a great example and that in use.

replies.author: @bluesbest1
replies.text: True. Even the actors swinging their blades, full-on humans without magic powers, still move too fast for the cameras to pick up properly. Add in the Force, and you get people moving faster than the speed of thought.

replies.author: @JonduGaming
replies.text: @bluesbest1 I think it was stated in a comic or novel, but Deathbattle brought up that Obi-Wan had a reaction time down to the nanoseconds. That is INASNE to think about. The fight on Mustafar, if it were sped up to lore accurate time, was probably only two or three minutes instead of its current 10+ runtime.

replies.author: @consolescrub4031
replies.text:
Ironically, I was thinking it would help mess with their opponent's precognition due to having significantly more possibilities but they would presumably need to use the force to redirect themselves in the air or they're actually reducing down the possible locations they could be in due to not being entirely free from physics. Assuming they can do quick direction and elevation changes like Yoda effectively opens up a lot of different possible avenues of attack. There's a definite parallel there with other precognition gifted magical fighters such as Mistborn. 

(Messing with your opponent's precognition is of course very handy because if they have too many possible outcomes to consider, they might not even realise where you're attacking until you've taken their hand off.)

replies.author: @noahsmith1053
replies.text: Its also honestly a way to incorporate close combat in a way that doesn't feel disingenuous to me. You'll sometimes see punches or kicks but you usually never see someone get their legs or arms chopped off during combat for trying it. But here its a good counter that doesn't overextend. Always love seeing it.

replies.author: @EllAntares
replies.text: It's odd  trend that every notable bad guy in Starwars had some respirator or closed helm. It's like they all were Bane wannabees.

replies.author: @ThunderAngel27
replies.text: @EllAntares A few points to make here. 1: Most villains in star wars generally don't use respirators or closed helms (Even though realistically they probably should). 2: The advantage that they can give in combat and hazardous situations while seemingly being not too hard to come by in universe to me makes it weird that more bad guys don't use one(But there are Meta/IRL reasons for this). 3: Cade Bane didn't need a respirator, he could breath perfectly normal but still used one for Intimidation, Combat and use in Zero Oxygen Environments. While Villians like Malgus and Vader needed either or a Respirator and Protective Helm to just survive daily; Never mind in a intense fight. 4: Such devices are potential weak spot especially if the person using one actually needs it for regular basic function which most villains in star wars do not. If they do they use one besides actually needing it to survive, its  usually for the same reasons Cade Bane did.

author: @avalon1007
text: I think one of the things to also consider when watching fictional sword fights (and fights in general), is "What does this fight tell me about the characters involved". While it might not always be practical, an overly-wide swing from a sith lord can speak to their need for dominance, overconfidence in their abilities, or even their overwhelming strength. While most fights don't do this either, some of the best fights in fictional media are not "Perfect Sword Fights" but are great at telling us about the characters.

author: @The_Cranky_Painter
text: Two little thing that I'm not sure are canon anymore since Disney took over? Lightsabers had a gyroscoping effect, which makes them naturally resist movement that you have to be trained to overcome, or have the force to counter. And when you "lock" together sabers near the hilt, the overlapping containment fields resists being pulled apart again, so you actually have to apply force to break the lock.

author: @Rex_Von_Umbra
text: @Shadiversity The reason you don’t see them deactivating and then reactivating their lightsabers in most lightsaber  fights is because it’s an actual lightsaber form called trakata but because it’s a lightsaber form that’s forbidden by both Jedi and Sith alike the Jedi don’t use it because it conflicted with their ideals of honor, honesty, and restraint, instead favoring transparency in combat. Both the Jedi and Sith considered it a deceitful and dishonorable tactic, though for different reasons. For the Sith, it was a sign of weakness, as they valued raw power and passion over trickery, while for the Jedi, it was an unethical approach that violated their commitment to preserving life.

author: @cory6266
text:
Always keep in mind for lightsaber battles!  It's not just a contest of swordsmanship, it's also the Force.  Both sides have Spider Sense, the Force guides them to the actions that will get the result they want, it's a matter of who is more in tune with what the Force is telling them, so they can see _farther ahead_ into the duel than the other guy, and out-precognition him.  

This is why in so many fights you'll see one guy not take advantage of seemingly obvious openings, the Force is telling him not to, OR the Force guided the other guy to do what he did and he _KNEW_ his opponent _wouldn't_ take advantage, they see it all before it happens.  Obi-Wan tells Luke that that's how it works in the training scene on the Millennium Falcon.

replies.author: @vinylwubs6987
replies.text: The force did it

replies.author: @guildsbounty283
replies.text:
I feel like this justification can go way too far. There's the precognizance of The Force as a mechanism for their duels...but then there's using "The Force Did It" as justification for sub-par choreography. And so I think the important distinction is this...

Did the choreographers do that on purpose? Or are we just excusing it after-the-fact?

The Force can justify showy combat, it can justify stunts (like behind-the-back parries, throwing your weapon, blind parries, acrobatics) that a normal person wouldn't reasonably attempt in a fight, it can justify not falling for feints and lures. But if the choreography has put the characters in a position where one has a completely open and clear shot at landing a hit...and they don't take it? Tighten up the choreography...don't lean of The Force to justify poor fight plotting.

replies.author: @wingsoffreedom3589
replies.text: ​@guildsbounty283this is a perfect take

replies.author: @leonthayne
replies.text: @guildsbounty283 Indeed. Duels are also a test of willpower. You know how the force can easily sway the weak-minded? Imagine being subjected to such efforts during your duel with another force-sensitive opponent. Both of you are also battling each other mentally, attempting to use the force to distract and destabilize each other. Lightsaber duels often take so long because they are essentially chess matches, and tests of mental endurance. The reason Obi-Wan was able to defeat Maul so fast is because he was able to correctly and completely trick him into thinking he was going to apply his old strategies. Maul was too blinded by revenge to sense the change. Whereas Obi-Wan's fight with Anakin was far more head-on, he was trying to wear Anakin down without lethally killing him.

replies.author: @cory6266
replies.text:
@guildsbounty283 
"Remember, a Jedi can feel the Force flowing through him."
"You mean it controls your actions?"
"Partially, but it also obeys your commands."
"I suggest you try again Luke, only this time, act on instinct."
And he proceeds to perfectly block three shots while blind.  This is literally the first thing Star Wars ever says about what the Force does.  It guides the Jedi's actions to achieve their goal.

PS, "showy combat" is justified by lightsabers, they're like capacitors for momentum, there is no such thing as a useless flourish.  When you actually know Star Wars, all these things are explained.

replies.author: @cory6266
replies.text: @leonthayne What you're describing is Dun Möch, a Sith method of undermining their enemy's focus.  You can see it work, and fail to work, in Revenge of the Sith.  Palpatine tried it on Mace Windu, but Vaapad (lightsaber Form 7, Windu's style of combat that he refined from the incomplete Juyo form, which was Maul's favorite) is a mirror for the dark side, that's why Palps was so mad.  But it does work on Yoda, that's why he was laughing.

author: @koendsatu8226
text: The fact that lightsabers have seven distinct forms, and that by the time you reach Jedi Master level you’ve usually specialized in just one form, is important — because unlike sword and armor combat, with a lightsaber, if you slip up once, you lose a limb or you’re done. For example, Obi-Wan is a master of a defensive specialization, but over time he learned the basics of Anakin’s style, and it still took him years to be able to use three different forms — with his first style always being the dominant one. In every duel, with every opponent, you never know which form they’ll use or what tricks they might have picked up from other styles, and you can’t let your guard down for even a second. The Old Republic, both in the cinematics and in the game, shows you that no matter how much you train, there’s always someone out there who can take you down in the blink of an eye.

author: @coreyvisser7004
text: When it comes to binding, in my opinion there are different rules for combat in different environments. In this case I think it more plausible and useful for its intended purpose. Malgus was trying to show dominance and get into his opponent's head. He's effectively saying it doesn't matter if you're fast enough to block my attacks because you're not strong enough to stop me. Feel the difference between our strength as I push through your guard. It wasn't just for cinematic reasons so they could exchange a few words mid-combat, it was part of their mental battle.

author: @GTOrphan
text:
Something to bare in mind with lightsaber combat is that they are not designed, or trained to fight against other melee weapons exclusively. They are often dodging, blocking and deflecting laser fire from a wide range of angles. The acrobatics and wide arc swings make them a far harder target against range attacks. There are combat forms designed for duelling, but in reality, melee vs melee combat forms would be the least beneficial to master as a whole, as Jedi Vs Sith combat was relatively rare. It was often Republic troops vs Imperial Soldiers with a force user as leader. In a galaxy spanning war that would have millions of active combatants, the Jedi and Sith numbered in the thousands.


Also, locking blades might be silly in reality, but a Sith warrior would seek to over power the Jedi in combat. Winning by raw strength and power is far more important then winning via tactics.

author: @Richard3rd665
text:
1:47 Trailers produced by Blur Studios (who made swtor ones) supposedly cost about $1 million usd per minute.
The Acolyte has a runtime of 329 minutes, and a budget of 230 million = $1.4 million usd per minute.

For the same budget we could have an entire season, or basically two whole movies of quality animation.

Adjusting for inflation the cost of 1$ million usd from 2010 would be about 1.48 million now

author: @CMVBrielman
text:
The duel over Korriban is my favorite of all of Star Wars. It has nearly everything I could ever ask for: dual wielding two-bladed lightsabers and tactically igniting/de-igniting the blades. (and that was done in the old EU by, IIRC, Corran Horn).

The only thing I could possibly want more would be asymmetrical length two-bladed sabers. I want to see a saber with one long blade for attack and one short blade for parrying. And then show a Jedi/Sith that dual-wields those.

author: @marcusaurelius5149
text:
6:27 - The behind flourish behind the back stab actually makes sense when you see the feinting.  Flourishing high overhead can lure your opponent's weapon away from a hidden strike.

Alex Pereira in the UFC and Glory would make a low kick behind his own leg with his rear leg to touch and unbalance his opponent's front foot.  Then he'd throw a hard right behind it if there's an opening.  The principle here is the same, only with a lightsaber it's far more deadly because just a little touch could be a killing blow.

author: @Vaderian88
text:
This was peek for Star Wars, they had just released The Force Unleashed and The Old Republic in  2008, the Expanded Universe incorporated these with new stories that tied them into the overall Star Wars mythos. But for those trailers if I remember right it was for the later expansions for the Old Republic, they were made by people who understood what Star Wars was and who their targeted audience was. The trailers felt like you were watching a mini movie before going in to play the game. What I also like I think there was a narrative story for the OR voiced by Lance Henriksen telling the story from a quick "this is what's happening if you don't know" perspective.

Also to talk about the Force Unleashed with Vader's Secret Apprentice Galen "Starkiller" Marek, played and voice by Sam Witwer, can be played either Light side or Dark side which does have effect over the course of the story and ending. Different and new Force powers and abilities, Starkiller holding his lightsaber behind him in the reverse grip stance, and plunging your blade into the space between the eyes of a rancor, while giving a blast of Force lightning. And again new stories to include it into the lore of Star Wars.
And who else pulls a Star Destroyer towards him on crash it into the ground while it was at full forward thrust??????

Yet for some weird reason from the mouth of KK there's nothing for source material for new stories!!!!


Man those were the days of Star Wars.... Oh I've still got this game on 2 different platforms to play it.

"Sacrifice" is straight up just a very short movie because it actually tells an entire story in 4.5 minutes. If you want to you can make a full 90 minute picture out of it and it would be an instant classic. Bioware back in the day was a beast

author: @boxhead77
text:
1:40 How much to animate this... The studio and director of these animations was Tim Miller and his Blur Studio. 
It would cost alot today cause they upgraded from doing computer game animations to full animated movies!    
TIm Miller is the executive producer of all the Sonic movies (and is on the fourth one). Love Death and Robots series. Secret Level. Deadpool... and he animated Clippy from MS Windows
Estimated budgets for Secret Levels was a 20 million budget, and Love Death Robots was 2 to 12 million per episode for fourth season considered the most expensive animated series ever produced.

author: @darthhuller5201
text:
Iirc the cost tag was almost one million dollars per minute but animation and computer technology has improved a lot since so it would likely be somewhat cheaper nowadays but still substantial. 
For reference, near the end of it's original run the Clone wars series was a million dollars per episode which was still considered too expensive to justify itself if Lucas didn't fund production out of pocket.

author: @BlackRomeo_v2
text:
9:45 That is a form of Lightsaber combat called Tràkata. It's the style that focuses on turning the saber on and off in combat. Jedi and Sith both view it as dishonorable. Jedi because it's not fair to be deceptive like that and Sith because it's a sign or weakness to win sneakily like that.

It works well here because he's using a single bladed saber and a dual bladed saber. With that setup, he can lure his opponent into the flow and momentum of the sabers just both being single bladed until he creates an opening. Even when he turned it off, he turned it off during a clash to force the Sith off balance.

The fights in these trailer we're crafted to be flashy but also efficient...in my humble layman's opinion.

author: @cordrevan-shepard3195
text: Love those trailers, also love the fight scene autopsies you've done, especially ROTS. I'd love to see a fight scene autopsy for the Duel of the Fates in episode 1. That's another great example of good swordsmanship and tactical fighting in star wars in my opinion.

author: @joshguyer4593
text:
All the best saber jockeys used Trakata. It's a variant of saber combat that focuses on trickery, And yes, they turn sabers on and off. The best users will sometime build their sabers with power settings so they can dial back the power so sabers will pass through it, just to turn the power back up. Trakata Is often time used with a double saber or duel wielded. 

Unfortunately, both the sith and jedi basically ban it's use. The jedi say it leads to the dark side because it's unsportsmanlike. The sith say it makes you weak.

replies.text:
Yeah the future of star wars isn't set in stone.  The sequels could easily have been a lost sith empire invading the start wars galaxy and Luke's jedi order leading a defense of it. So you have tons of lukes students leading the defense of the republic, and luke occasionally showing up to do epic grandmaster stuff.

It could have been just like this.  But no we had to get Rey...
